coredata.dc:title: A signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on the integration of spherical nucleic acid and CRISPR/Cas12a for multiplex detection of HIV/HPV DNAs
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Kai-Ren
Wang, Li
Liu, Peng-Fei
Hang, Xiao-Min
Wang, Hui-Yi
Ye, Shu-Ying
Liu, Zhi-Jun
Liang, Guo-Xi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for simple, sensitive and quantitative detection of two kinds of virus genes, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and human papilloma virus (HPV-16) DNAs, by the integration of spherical nucleic acid (SNA) with CRISPR/Cas12a. The concept behind this approach is “on” signal based on a sandwich type assay after ECL luminophores of carbon dots (CDs) labeled SNA incubated with the biosensor in the presence of HIV DNA; and “off” signal upon the biosensor incubation with assistant DNA (HIV DNA sequence), CDs-labeled SNA and Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 DNA ternary complex relying on the collaterally cleavage of Cas12a. The cross-interference between HIV and HPV-16 DNAs can be avoided by adding Cas12a effector to the system or not. Using this strategy, HIV and HPV-16 DNAs are successfully quantitatively detection with detection limits of 30 fM and 0.32 pM (S/N = 3), respectively. Importantly, this system does not rely on multiplex signal reporting molecules, thus simplifying the detection system and avoiding the likelihood of cross interferences presented in the assay. Furthermore, multiplex detection of HIV/HPV-16 DNAs in human serum samples are successfully achieved within 2 h without additional nucleic acid amplification step. As demonstrated, the proposed biosensor has the potential for multiplex detection in clinical diagnosis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence
Cas12a
Spherical nucleic acid
HIV
HPV

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the stepwise fabrication of the biosensor
Multiplex detection of HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA
Detection of spiked HIV/HPV-16 DNAs in human serum and swab samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (A) AuNPs, (B) SNA and (C) CDs-labeled SNA, (D) UV–vis spectrum of AuNPs, SNA, CDs-labeled SNA.

(A) ECL and (B) EIS characterization of the stepwise fabrication process of the ECL biosensor: (a) bare GE, (b) capture DNA/GE, (c) MCH/capture DNA/GE, (d) HIV DNA/MCH/capture DNA/GE, (e) CDs-labeled SNA/HIV DNA/MCH/capture DNA/GE and (f) CDs-labeled SNA/HIV DNA/MCH/capture DNA/GE treated with Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 DNA triplex.

Optimization of the experimental conditions: (A) the capture DNA concentration, (B) the incubation time of HIV DNA and CDs-labeled SNA, (C) the concentration ratio of probe DNA and report DNA, (D) the cleavage time of Cas12a.

(A) ECL curves responding to 0, 100 fM, 1 pM, 10 pM, 100 pM, 1 nM, 10 nM, 100 nM, and 1 μM HIV DNA. (B) Linear fitting of the ΔECL and the logarithmic concentration of HIV DNA. (C) ECL curves responding to 0, 1 pM, 5 pM, 10 pM, 50 pM, 100 pM, 500 pM, 1 nM, 5 nM, and 100 nM HPV-16 DNA. (D) Linear fitting of the ΔECL and the logarithmic concentration of HPV-16 DNA.

(A) Specificity of HIV DNA detection. (all investigated DNAs concentrations are 1 nM). (B) Specificity of HPV-16 DNA detection. (all investigated DNAs concentrations are 1 nM). (C) Reproducibility of HIV DNA detection. (D) Reproducibility of HPV-16 DNA detection.

Schematic illustration of the “on-off” signal-switchable ECL biosensor for HIV/HPV DNAs detection by combination of CDs-labeled SNA and Cas12a. (A) The preparation of CDs-labeled SNA. (B) The detection of HIV DNA based on a sandwich-type DNA hybridization strategy. CDs-labeled SNA are captured on the electrode by HIV DNA. (C) The detection of HPV-16 DNA based on Cas12a collaterally cleaves ssDNA reporter sequence from CDs-labeled SNA.
Comparison of the CRISPR-based ECL biosensor for HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA assay with other work.

head.ce:title: A signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence biosensor based on the integration of spherical nucleic acid and CRISPR/Cas12a for multiplex detection of HIV/HPV DNAs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang, 212013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of the Environment and Safety Engineering, Jiangsu University, Zhenjiang 212013, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for simple, sensitive and quantitative detection of two kinds of virus genes, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) and human papilloma virus (HPV-16) DNAs, by the integration of spherical nucleic acid (SNA) with CRISPR/Cas12a. The concept behind this approach is “on” signal based on a sandwich type assay after ECL luminophores of carbon dots (CDs) labeled SNA incubated with the biosensor in the presence of HIV DNA; and “off” signal upon the biosensor incubation with assistant DNA (HIV DNA sequence), CDs-labeled SNA and Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 DNA ternary complex relying on the collaterally cleavage of Cas12a. The cross-interference between HIV and HPV-16 DNAs can be avoided by adding Cas12a effector to the system or not. Using this strategy, HIV and HPV-16 DNAs are successfully quantitatively detection with detection limits of 30 fM and 0.32 pM (S/N = 3), respectively. Importantly, this system does not rely on multiplex signal reporting molecules, thus simplifying the detection system and avoiding the likelihood of cross interferences presented in the assay. Furthermore, multiplex detection of HIV/HPV-16 DNAs in human serum samples are successfully achieved within 2 h without additional nucleic acid amplification step. As demonstrated, the proposed biosensor has the potential for multiplex detection in clinical diagnosis.

ce:section.ce:para: Clustered regularly spaced short palindrome repeats (CRISPR)/Cas system, as an adaptive immune system against invading nucleic acid components in bacteria and archaea, has been extensively deployed for gene-editing [1–3]. In addition to remarkable gene-editing ability, the CRISPR system also shows outstanding competence in the field of nucleic acid sensing [4–8]. In particular, the Cas proteins including Cas12a, Cas13a, and Cas14 were recently discovered have a collateral cleavage activity, which endows these CRISPR/Cas systems with efficient self-signal amplification capability and opens new way for molecular diagnosis [9–12]. By using Cas12a, Cas13a, or Cas14, several CRISPR/Cas nucleic acids diagnostic systems include SHERLOCK [13–15], DETECTR [9], and HOLMES [16] have been developed with extremely high sensitivity (single-molecule levels) as well as high specific, simple, fast, and robust performance. These advantages make CRISPR/Cas as an ideal technique for development of next-generation diagnostic biosensing platforms.

In recent years, multiplex detection is of increasing interest in many fields such as clinical diagnosis [17–19], environmental monitoring [20–22] and food control [23,24] for more reliable information. Performing multiplex detection, however, is still challenging by the limited signal reporting strategies, compromised sensitivity, possible cross-reactions, and interference between recognition molecules and various analytes [25–28]. As a newly emerged technology, CRISPR/Cas multiplex detection utilization is even more scarce. Indeed, collateral cleavage activity imparts CRISPR/Cas assays with advantages of highly sensitive, specific, rapid, and cost-efficient. Nevertheless, for multianalyte detection, unspecific cutting makes the differentiation of analytes signals nonviable. Currently, only a few CRISPR/Cas systems were used for multiplex detection [29,30]. A successful example is the work presented by Gootenberg and colleagues. In that study, they analyzed four targets relying on screening out four distinguished orthogonal Cas effectors (PsmCas13b, LwaCas13a, CcaCas13b, and AsCas12a) with cutting preferences to different reporter nucleotide sequences [13]. However, the introduction of multiplex Cas effectors not only adds the system’s complexity, but also increases the likelihood of cross interferences [31]. Additionally, almost all CRISPR methods rely on an additional nucleic acids amplification process to improve the detection sensitivity. However, nucleic acids amplification is not ideal for multiplex detection owing to the interferences between different primer and probe sets [31,32].

In this work, we integrated CRISPR/Cas system with spherical nucleic acid (SNA) to fabricate a “on-off” signal-switchable electrochemiluminescence (ECL) biosensor for the detection of dual biomarkers by using a single Cas12a effector. Here, we choose Cas12a for detection mainly because: compared with Cas13a, the Cas12a-based sensing method does not require additional steps to convert the amplified DNA back to RNA, and does not require the use of unstable and expensive RNA reporter genes; compared with the newly discovered Cas14, Cas12a has been studied in more diagnostic applications [33,34]. Two common sexually transmitted viruses, human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) [35,36] and human papilloma viruses (HPV) [37], were used to demonstrate potential application of the ECL biosensor since HPV infection can increase the risk of HIV acquisition [38], and vice versa [39,40]. This method consisted of performing two separate ECL detection procedures: the “on” ECL signal was obtained after incubating the biosensor with HIV DNA and ECL luminophores of carbon dots (CDs)-labeled SNA; the “off” ECL signal was obtained after incubating the biosensor with assistant DNA, CDs-labeled SNA and Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 dsDNA complex. The cross-interference between HIV and HPV-16 DNAs was avoided by adding Cas12a effector to the system or not. The biosensor yielded the sensitivities of 30 fM for HIV DNA and 0.32 pM for HPV-16 DNA, respectively, without further amplification. Moreover, the feasibility of the proposed ECL biosensor was explored for HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA human serum detection to demonstrate the potential for clinical application.

Tris(2-carboxyethyl)phosphine hydrochloride (TCEP) and 6-Mercapto-1-hexanol (MCH) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO. USA). Citric acid and urea were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Polyethylene glycol sorbitan monolaurate (Tween-20), hydrogen tetrachloroaurate(III) trihydrate (HAuCl4·3H2O), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) and hydroxylsuccinimide sodium salt (NHS) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). EnGen®Lba Cas12a and 10 × NEB buffer 2.1 were obtained from New England Biolabs (Ipswich, MA, USA). The polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) gel preparation kit and all oligonucleotides (Table S1) were offered by Sangon Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Ultrapure water was used throughout this study.

UV–vis absorption spectra were determined by a UV-3600 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were investigated by a JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope. An ECL instrument (Xi’an Remex Analysis Instruments Co., Ltd., China) was employed to record the ECL signal with a three-electrode system, including a GE (modified gold electrode) working electrode, an Ag/AgCl reference electrode and a platinum wire counter electrode, respectively. Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.1 M, pH = 7.4) (K2HPO4-KH2PO4-KCl) containing 0.1 M K2S2O8 was used as ECL working solution with the potential scanning from 0 to -1.6 V and the photomultiplier tube was set at 800 V.

The citrate-gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) (13 nm, 17 nM) were prepared following the previously reported literature [41]. And the CDs were synthesized by a microwave-assisted method, according to our published work [42].

The SNA was constructed through a freezing protocol with AuNPs, probe DNA and report DNA. The thiolated probe DNA (1 μM, 10 μL) and report DNA (20 μM, 10 μL) were first mixed with 100 μL 13 nm-citrate-AuNPs. Then tween-20 (1%, w/w) was added to a final concentration of 0.05 %. The mixture was frozen at −20 °C for 2 h. After thawing at room temperature, the solution was centrifuged at 12 000 rpm for 5 min and washed three times with 10 mM Tris-HCl to obtain SNA. The prepared SNA was resuspended in 25 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) for further use.

The CDs-labeled SNA was prepared using a carbodiimide method. Briefly, 40 μL CDs was activated by adding an aqueous mixture of EDC/NHS (80/20 mg/mL) with gently shaking for 2 h at room temperature. Afterwards, 100 μL SNA was added to the above solution, and incubated for 12 h to generate CDs-labeled SNA. The excessive unreacted CDs was removed by centrifugation at 12 000 rpm for 5 min. After washed thrice with 10 mM Tris-HCl, the CDs-labeled SNA was resuspended in 25 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4) and stored at 4 °C until use.

Prior to use, the GE (Φ = 3 mm) was polished with alumina powder (1, 0.3, and 0.05 μm), followed by washing ultrasonically with ethanol and ultrapure water to gain mirror-like surface. Subsequently, the polished electrode was electrochemically cleaned by cyclic scanning in the potential range from 0 to +1.5 V in 0.5 M H2SO4 to remove remaining impurities. Straight after, the capture DNA (pretreated with 10 mM TCEP) (1 μM, 4 μL) was dropped on the cleaned electrode surface and incubated overnight at 4 °C. A blocking treatment to eliminate the nonspecific adsorption was carried out by dipping the modified GE in 1 mM MCH for 1 h. After rinsed with ultrapure water, the modificatory biosensor was stored at 4 °C for further experiment.

For HIV DNA detection, a mixture containing 4 μL different concentrations of HIV DNA and 5 μL CDs-labeled SNA were dropped onto the biosensor surface and incubated at an ambient temperature for 50 min. After washed with PBS, the biosensor was transferred to 5 mL ECL working solution for signal measurements. For HPV DNA detection, the biosensor was pre-incubated with assistant DNA (HIV sequence) (1 μM, 4 μL) and 5 μL CDs-labeled SNA for 50 min. After washed with ultrapure water, 10 μL reaction system containing 5 μL HPV-16 DNA at different concentration and 5 μL Cas12a-crRNA duplex (50 nM Cas12a and 100 nM crRNA) were dropped to biosensor surface and incubated at 37 °C 1 h. The biosensor was washed with ultrapure water and transferred to ECL working solution for measurement.

The nanostructures of AuNPs, SNA, CDs and CDs-labeled SNA are first characterized by TEM. From Fig. 1 A–C, it can be seen that the average diameters of AuNPs, SNA, and CDs-labeled SNA are all about 13 nm, while the monodispersity of AuNPs is improved gradually after functionalized with DNA and CDs. The TEM image of CDs is shown in Fig. S1, we can see that the size of CDs is less than 5 nm. The dramatic change in the zeta potential (Fig. S2) also indicates the successful immobilization of the DNA and CDs on the surface of AuNPs. In addition, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy provides direct evidence as well. As depicted in Fig. 1D, a surface plasmon resonance peak of the AuNPs at around 520 nm, a characteristic absorption peak of nucleic acids at 260 nm, and a characteristic absorption peak of CDs at 410 nm are all observed in CDs-labeled SNA. All the results demonstrate the successful synthesis of CDs-labeled SNA.

We used CRISPR/Cas12a to combine SNA to build a signal-switchable ECL biosensor for multiplex HIV/HPV-16 DNAs detection. As shown in Scheme 1 A, before DNAs detection, the SNA is prepared by assembling lots of report DNA and few probe DNA on a ∼13 nm gold nanoparticle (AuNP) through the gold-thiol bond. Amino groups are modified on the far end of the probe DNA, which can conjugate ECL luminophores CDs via the subsequent EDC/NHS reaction. The principle of “on-off” signal-switchable ECL biosensor for multiplex HIV/HPV-16 DNAs detection is shown in Scheme 1B and C. As illustrated, this method consists of performing two separate ECL detection procedures: the “on” ECL signal is obtained after incubation the biosensor with HIV DNA and CDs-labeled SNA via DNA hybridization forming a sandwiched DNA structure (Scheme 1B); the “off” ECL signal is obtained after incubation the biosensor with assistant DNA (HIV DNA sequence), CDs-labeled SNA and Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 dsDNA complex relying on the trans-cleavage ssDNA of Cas12a effector (Scheme 1C). The cross-interference of HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA can be successful avoided by adding Cas12a effector to the system or not.

To prove the feasibility of the biosensor, the polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE) characterization is conducted in Fig. S3. Lane 1–3 shows the process of HIV DNA connecting the capture DNA and the probe DNA. The new bands at low migrations (lane 2 and lane 3) indicate the stepwise formation of the combined structure (lane 3). Lane 4–7 shows the mixture of Cas12a, crRNA, HPV-16 and ssDNA at different conditions. For the verification of cleavage of report DNA by Cas12a, a 39 nt ssDNA is used as the nonspecific substrate. It can be seen only in lane 7, the random ssDNA is degraded into small fragments, and therefore does not appear as clear lines in the gel. On the contrary, in the absence of Cas12a (lane 4), crRNA (lane 5) or target HPV-16 DNA (lane 6) respectively, random ssDNA can not be degraded because the Cas12a is not be active.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the stepwise fabrication of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: ECL measurements are first performed to identify the stepwise fabrication processes of the biosensor (Fig. 2 A). The bare GE shows a weak ECL signal. With capture DNA, MCH, and HIV DNA coating onto the bare GE surface successively, sluggish ECL signal is also gained (curve b, c and d). After CDs-labeled SNA is dropped onto the modified electrode surface, a remarkable increase of ECL intensity is obtained (curve e), which is ascribed to the reaction between the CDs and K2S2O8. After the biosensor further incubates with Cas12a-crRNA-HPV-16 DNA triplex, the ECL intensity (curve f) prominently diminishes since the report DNA is cleaved by Cas12a. The results verify the successful stepwise modification of biosensor.

The stepwise surface modification of the GE is further characterization by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). As revealed in Fig. 2B, the bare GE shows a nearly straight line which means a low electron transfer resistance (Ret) (curve a). With the modification of capture DNA, the Ret shows a moderate increase that ascribed to the electronic repulsion between Fe(CN)6 3−/4− and negatively charged DNA (curve b). When the nonconductive MCH is modified on the biosensor surface, the Ret further increases (curve c). The introduction of HIV DNA causes the Ret sequentially increase (curve d). After CDs-labeled SNA bioconjugates are assembled onto the biosensor and formed a sandwiched DNA structure, a sharply enhanced Ret (curve e) is observed. The phenomenon is attributed to that massive DNA on the AuNPs greatly hamper the electron transfer. However, in the presence of the Cas12a/crRNA/HPV-16 DNA triplex, the Ret significantly decreases (curve f). The reason for this result is that the report- DNA is cut into fragments by Cas12a. These results are in conformity with ECL, suggesting the biosensor is fabricated successfully.

For the best performance, the important experimental conditions are optimized. The concentration of capture DNA on the electrode surface is primarily investigated. As shown in Fig. 3 A, the ECL intensity gradually increases with increasing concentration of capture DNA from 0.1 μM to 1 μM and reaches a plateau. Therefore, the concentration of capture DNA is selected at 1 μM. The incubation time of HIV DNA and CDs-labeled SNA is also evaluated. As seen in Fig. 3B, the ECL signal increases from 10 to 50 min and then reaches a platform. Thus, 50 min is chosen as an appropriate incubation time. The ratio of probe DNA and report DNA, which is highly relative with the ECL performance of the biosensor, is optimized. As revealed in Fig. 3C, the ECL intensity reaches the maximum at the ratio of probe DNA and report DNA of 1:20, which is determined as the optimal condition. Fig. 3D describes that as the cleavage time increases, the ECL signal sharply declines before 60 min and slightly attenuates after that, indicating that the trans-cleavage of Cas12a is a time-dependent procedure. As a trade-off between timeliness and sensitivity, 60 min is identified as a suitable cleavage time for Cas12a.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multiplex detection of HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the specificity of the ECL biosensor, several other types of interfering DNA were randomly detected. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the comparative detections for HIV are conducted by using HAV, HBV, HPV-16 and HPV-18 DNA to replace HIV (1 nM), respectively. As expected, there are low ECL responses with the interfering agents and blank control, while the HIV DNA and the mixture with HIV DNA manifest a remarkable ECL intensity. When HPV-16 DNA (1 nM) and the mixture containing HPV-16 DNA is incubated with the Cas12a-crRNA duplex the ECL intensity sharply decreases compared to those interfering agents as illustrated in Fig. 5B. The results indicates that the biosensor have excellent specificity.

Subsequently, the reproducibility of this biosensor is assessed by analyzing HIV DNA (100 fM and 10 pM) and HPV-16 DNA (5 pM and 100 pM) in five parallel experiments, respectively. As seen in Fig. 5C and D, the relative standard deviation (RSD) is around 2.1 % and 1.3 % for HIV DNA and 1.4 % and 2.2 % for HPV-16 DNA, suggesting that the proposed biosensor have dependable reproducibility.

Moreover, the stability of biosensor was assessed by storing it at 4 °C for different days. The corresponding results are displayed in Fig. S4. After 10 days of store, the ECL intensity have a slight fluctuation when the biosensors were incubated with different concentrations of HIV DNA and HPV-16 DNA. These results indicated that the biosensor have an excellent stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of spiked HIV/HPV-16 DNAs in human serum and swab samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of the ECL biosensor, HIV and HPV-16 DNAs at various concentrations is spiked in normal human serum (obtained from the Jiangsu University Staff Hospital, China), were examined. As listed in Table S2, the satisfactory recoveries ranged from 93.0 to 101.0 % with the RSD between 2.2–4.2 % for HIV DNA and 95.5–101.1 % with the RSD of 2.4–4.5 % for HPV-16 DNA detection, are obtained.

The clinical concentration of HPV DNA in the serum can be lower [50,51] and therefore we demonstrate our biosensor with detection of HPV-16 DNA in swab samples (Clinical cervical swab samples were obtained from the Jiangsu University Staff Hospital, China) by purifying DNA from the sample using sample preparation as described in the literature [34]. The recoveries data demonstrates that HPV-16 DNA in the swab samples is also satisfied (Table S3). These results suggest a prominent practical application for HIV/HPV-16 DNAs multiplex detection in real samples, which exhibits a great potential for clinical diagnostics.

In summary, we developed a novel signal-switchable ECL biosensor for multiplex detection of HIV/HPV-16 DNAs on the basis of using spherical nucleic acid (SNA) and CRISPR/Cas12a. The concept of this approach is that the presence of target HIV DNA connects the labels-loaded SNA to the electrode forming a sandwich structure, results in an increased ECL signal (“on signal”); target HPV-16 DNA triggers the trans-cleavage activity of Cas12a effector towards single-strand DNA, leading to ECL signal decrease (“off signal”). By integrating labels-loaded SNA and CRISPR/Cas12a to the ECL assay platform, it is possible to detect HIV DNA as low as 30 fM and detect HPV-16 DNA as low as 0.32 pM within 2 h without additional nucleic acid amplification step. Importantly, this system does not rely on multiplex Cas protein effectors, the interference between HIV and HPV-16 DNAs can be avoided by only adding Cas12a effector to the system. Furthermore, this biosensor may be easily combined with isothermal amplification methods (e.g., the recombinase polymerase amplification (RPA) and loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP)) to achieve ultra-sensitive multiple detection. However, the detection of ssDNA bring inconvenience to the development of Cas-based multiplex ECL biosensor. Further simplifying the detection condition will facilitate the direct application of this novel biosensor in clinical diagnostic.

Kai-Ren Zhao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Li Wang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation, Funding acquisition. Peng-Fei Liu: Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - review & editing. Xiao-Min Hang: Software, Validation, Visualization. Hui-Yi Wang: Software, Validation, Visualization. Shu-Ying Ye: Software, Validation, Visualization. Zhi-Jun Liu: Software, Validation, Visualization. Guo-Xi Liang: Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21707053) and Senior Talent Foundation of Jiangsu University (No. 10JDG052, 10JDG038).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2021.130485.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One-step generation of mice carrying mutations in multiple genes by CRISPR/Cas-mediated genome engineering

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, H. Yang, C.S. Shivalila, M.M. Dawlaty, A.W. Cheng, F. Zhang, R. Jaenisch, One-step generation of mice carrying mutations in multiple genes by CRISPR/Cas-mediated genome engineering, Cell 153 (2013) 910-918.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR-mediated live imaging of genome editing and transcription

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, M. Nakamura, T.R. Abbott, D. Zhao, K. Luo, C. Yu, C.M. Nguyen, A. Lo, T.P. Daley, M.L. Russa, Y. Liu, L.S. Qi, CRISPR-mediated live imaging of genome editing and transcription. Science 365 (2019) 1301-1305.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR-Cpf1 assisted genome editing of corynebacterium glutamicum

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Jiang, F. Qian, J. Yang, Y. Liu, F. Dong, C. Xu, B. Sun, B. Chen, X. Xu, Y. Li, R. Wang, S. Yang, CRISPR-Cpf1 assisted genome editing of corynebacterium glutamicum. Nat. Commun. 8 (2017) 15179.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An integrated multi-function heterogeneous biochemical circuit for high-resolution electrochemistry-based genetic analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Dai, W. Xu, R.A. Somoza, J.F. Welter, A.I. Caplan, C.C. Liu, An integrated multi-function heterogeneous biochemical circuit for high-resolution electrochemistry-based genetic analysis. Angew. Chem. Int. Ed. 59 (2020) 20545-20551.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR/Cas12a-based dual amplified biosensing system for sensitive and rapid detection of polynucleotide kinase/phosphatase

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D.-X. Wang, J. Wang, Y.-C. Du, J.-Y. Ma, S.-Y. Wang, A.-N. Tang, D.-M. Kong, CRISPR/Cas12a-based dual amplified biosensing system for sensitive and rapid detection of polynucleotide kinase/phosphatase. Biosens. Bioelectron. 168 (2020) 112556.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR/Cas13a powered portable electrochemiluminescence chip for ultrasensitive and specific miRNA detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Zhou, R. Huang, M. Huang, J. Shen, Y. Shan, D. Xing, CRISPR/Cas13a powered portable electrochemiluminescence chip for ultrasensitive and specific miRNA detection. Adv. Sci. 7 (2020) 1903661.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cas12a-based electrochemiluminescence biosensor for target amplification-free DNA detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.-F. Liu, K.-R. Zhao, Z.-J. Liu, L. Wang, S.-Y. Ye, G.-X. Liang, Cas12a-based electrochemiluminescence biosensor for target amplification-free DNA detection. Biosens. Bioelectron. 176 (2021) 112954.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive CRISPR/Cas12a based electrochemical biosensor for listeria monocytogenes detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Li, Q. Ye, M. Chen, B. Zhou, J. Zhang, R. Pang, L. Xue, J. Wang, H. Zeng, S. Wu, Y. Zhang, Y. Ding, Q. Wu, An ultrasensitive CRISPR/Cas12a based electrochemical biosensor for listeria monocytogenes detection. Biosens. Bioelectron.179 (2021) 113073.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR-Cas12a target binding unleashes indiscriminate single-stranded DNase activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.S. Chen, E. Ma, L.B. Harrington, M. Da Costa, X. Tian, J.M. Palefsky, J.A. Doudna, CRISPR-Cas12a target binding unleashes indiscriminate single-stranded DNase activity. Science 360 (2018) 436-439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR-Cas12a has both cis- and trans-cleavage activities on single-stranded DNA

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRISPR/Cas12a-mediated interfacial cleaving of hairpin DNA reporter for electrochemical nucleic acid sensing

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Zhang, Y. Yan, H. Que, T. Yang, X. Cheng, S. Ding, X. Zhang, W. Cheng, CRISPR/Cas12a-mediated interfacial cleaving of hairpin DNA reporter for electrochemical nucleic acid sensing. ACS Sens. 5 (2020) 557-562. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.9b02461.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Kai-Ren Zhao is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Li Wang received her MS degree and Ph.D degree from ShaanXi Normal University. She is currently working at Department of Pharmacy, Jiangsu University. Her current research interests include multifunctional nanomaterial and their applications in diagnosis, metastasis, and treatment of cancer.

Peng-Fei Liu is presently pursuing his master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. His current research focuses on biosensors and their applications in cancer cell research and drug evaluation.

Xiao-Min Hang is presently pursuing her master degree in biology and medicine in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Hui-Yi Wang is presently pursuing her master degree in biology and medicine in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Shu-Ying Ye is presently pursuing her master degree in environmental engineering in Jiangsu University. Her research interests include development of biosensors and advanced materials for various purposes.

Zhi-Jun Liu is presently pursuing her master degree in pharmaceutical engineering in Jiangsu University. Her main research interest regards functional nanomaterials for biomedical and diagnostic applications.

Guo-Xi Liang received his Ph.D. degree from Nanjing University. He is currently working at Department of Environment Science, Jiangsu University. His current research interests include nanobiosensor and nanofabrication.
